16.1.title: अभयम् opens the daivī sampad litany
16.1.body: The chapter begins with a string of nominatives in apposition — अभयम्, सत्त्वसंशुद्धिः, ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थितिः — each प्रथमा एकवचन, parallel subjects sharing the eventual finite verb भवन्ति in 16.3. The very first word अभयम् (fearlessness) signals that the divine wealth is not ritual purity alone but a settled inner condition: ज्ञानयोगव्यवस्थिति, abiding-firmness in the yoga of knowledge.

16.1.title: दानं दमः यज्ञः — outer triad of daivī sampad
16.1.body: After the inner triad (अभय, संशुद्धि, व्यवस्थिति), Kṛṣṇa names the outer triad: दानम् (giving), दमः (self-restraint), यज्ञः (sacrifice), followed by स्वाध्यायः, तपः, आर्जवम्. Each is a नपुंसकलिङ्ग or पुल्लिङ्ग nominative listed without a verb — सूत्र-style enumeration where the predicate is held back for the third verse, mimicking the gathering of qualities into a single sampad.

16.2.title: अहिंसा सत्यम् — the ethical pādas of the list
16.2.body: Verse two continues the list with the classical yamas: अहिंसा (non-violence), सत्यम् (truth), अक्रोधः (non-anger), त्यागः (renunciation), शान्तिः (peace). Each remains प्रथमा एकवचन, syntactically suspended until 16.3 — the grammar itself enacts patience, holding twenty-six predicates aloft before the verb resolves them.

16.2.title: ह्रीः and अचापलम् — the quieter virtues
16.2.body: The verse closes with दया, अलोलुप्त्वम् (non-greed), मार्दवम् (gentleness), ह्रीः (modest shame at wrong), अचापलम् (non-fickleness). अलोलुप्त्वम् is a tad-dhita abstract from लोलुप — Sanskrit forms abstract nouns by adding -त्व, and the negation अ- creates the precise virtue: not merely 'absence of greed' but the quality of non-graspingness.

16.3.title: भवन्ति resolves the suspended list
16.3.body: Finally the finite verb arrives: भवन्ति (√भू, लट्, प्रथम पुरुष, बहुवचन) — 'they become' or 'they belong to'. All the nominatives from 16.1-2 plus तेजः, क्षमा, धृतिः, शौचम्, अद्रोहः, नातिमानिता now find their predicate. The षष्ठी अभिजातस्य ('of one born to') governs सम्पदं दैवीम् (द्वितीया) — these qualities accrue to the daivī-sampad-born.

16.3.title: नातिमानिता — the negated abstract
16.3.body: नातिमानिता is a layered formation: न + अति + मानिता — 'non-excessive-self-regard'. The -ता suffix builds the abstract noun from the agent मानिन्, and the doubled negation (न and the अति itself being qualified out) gives Sanskrit's characteristic precision: not just humility, but the absence of *excessive* pride, leaving room for legitimate self-respect.

16.4.title: Six āsurī attributes — a deliberately short list
16.4.body: Against the twenty-six divine qualities, Kṛṣṇa names only six demonic ones: दम्भः (hypocrisy), दर्पः (arrogance), अभिमानः (self-conceit), क्रोधः (anger), पारुष्यम् (harshness), अज्ञानम् (ignorance). The asymmetry is itself a teaching — virtue is many-faced and cultivated, while vice has few root forms that proliferate. All six remain प्रथमा, awaiting the implied predicate भवन्ति from the previous verse, parallelism by syntactic echo.

16.4.title: अभिजातस्य repeated — same construction, opposite content
16.4.body: The षष्ठी अभिजातस्य returns verbatim from 16.3, now governing सम्पदम् आसुरीम् instead of दैवीम्. The grammatical parallelism is exact — only the adjective flips. Sanskrit uses identical syntactic frames to highlight the moral mirror: same birth-construction, opposite inheritance.

16.5.title: विमोक्षाय / निबन्धाय — the dative of purpose
16.5.body: The two sampads are assigned destinies through चतुर्थी विभक्ति: दैवी विमोक्षाय (for liberation), आसुरी निबन्धाय (for bondage). The dative-of-purpose construction is grammatically minimal but theologically total — case ending alone decides eschatology. मता (past passive participle, स्त्रीलिङ्ग to match सम्पद्) carries the unspoken 'is regarded as'.

16.5.title: मा शुचः — the prohibitive aorist
16.5.body: मा शुचः is the classical निषेधार्थ construction: मा + लुङ् (aorist) without augment, here from √शुच् (to grieve). Sanskrit reserves मा + aorist specifically for prohibitions — 'do not grieve'. Kṛṣṇa reassures Arjuna with the same grammatical mood used in 2.3's मा स्म गमः, the imperative of the heart.

16.6.title: द्वौ भूतसर्गौ — the dual classification
16.6.body: द्वौ is प्रथमा द्विवचन of द्वि, matching भूतसर्गौ (the two creations of beings). Sanskrit's dual is alive here — Kṛṣṇa is not enumerating two among many but pairing them as a binary: दैवः and आसुरः, with एव locking the exhaustion ('only these two').

16.6.title: शृणु — the listener's imperative
16.6.body: शृणु (√श्रु, लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन) directly addresses Arjuna: 'hear from me' (मे, षष्ठी). The verse pivots on this command: the daivī has been described विस्तरशः (adverb in -शस्, 'in detail'); now the आसुरम् will receive the same treatment. The imperative marks the chapter's hinge.

16.7.title: प्रवृत्ति निवृत्ति — what the demonic don't know
16.7.body: जना न विदुः आसुराः — विदुः is परस्मैपद, लट्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन of √विद् ('they know'). The objects प्रवृत्तिम् and निवृत्तिम् (द्वितीया एकवचन) are the technical pair: when to engage and when to withdraw. The demonic lack precisely this discriminative knowledge that all śāstra exists to impart.

16.7.title: Triple न — the rhetoric of absence
16.7.body: The second hemistich strings three negations: न शौचम्, न आचारः, न सत्यम् — purity, conduct, truth. विद्यते (आत्मनेपद, लट्) is here the natural form of √विद् 'to be / be found' (divādigaṇa, sense सत्तायाम्) — not the passive of √विद् 'to know'. It takes तेषु (सप्तमी, 'in them') as locus. The piling negations enact the void itself: nothing is found in them where the divine qualities resided.

16.8.title: असत्यम् अप्रतिष्ठम् अनीश्वरम् — the world they describe
16.8.body: Three negated adjectives qualify जगत् (द्वितीया एकवचन नपुंसकलिङ्ग): without truth, without foundation, without a Lord. आहुः (परस्मैपद, लट्, 'they declare') is the verb of dogmatic assertion. Demonic metaphysics is defined here not by what it affirms but by what it strips away — the three negations dismantle ontology, ethics, and theology in one breath.

16.8.title: कामहैतुकम् — pleasure as first cause
16.8.body: अपरस्परसम्भूतम् ('mutually arisen' — bahuvrīhi compound) and कामहैतुकम् ('having desire as its cause' — tad-dhita with हेतु + ठक्) name the alternative cosmology: the world arises from random coupling, driven only by desire. किम् अन्यत् ('what else?') is rhetorical — for the demonic, lust is metaphysical bedrock.

16.9.title: एतां दृष्टिम् अवष्टभ्य — clinging to a view
16.9.body: अवष्टभ्य is an absolutive (ल्यप् after अव-prefix) from √स्तम्भ् — 'having grasped/propped themselves on'. The accusative एतां दृष्टिम् (this view from 16.8) is the support they clutch. Sanskrit's absolutive sequences action: first they cling, then they emerge (प्रभवन्ति) as उग्रकर्माणः (bahuvrīhi: 'those whose actions are fierce').

16.9.title: क्षयाय जगतः — dative of destruction
16.9.body: Once again चतुर्थी विभक्ति carries the purpose: क्षयाय ('for the destruction'), with जगतः (षष्ठी) specifying 'of the world'. नष्टात्मानः (bahuvrīhi: 'those whose self is lost') and अहिताः ('inimical, doing no good') frame them — already self-destroyed, they arise only to destroy.

16.10.title: दुष्पूरम् कामम् — the unfillable desire
16.10.body: दुष्पूरम् (दुस् + पूर, 'hard to fill') qualifies कामम् in द्वितीया. आश्रित्य is absolutive ('having taken refuge in'). The image is sharp: they have made desire itself their refuge, and that refuge is by definition bottomless. The bahuvrīhi दम्भमानमदान्विताः ('endowed with hypocrisy-pride-intoxication') names the three companions they carry into that refuge.

16.10.title: असद्ग्राहान् गृहीत्वा — having seized false grasps
16.10.body: गृहीत्वा (absolutive of √ग्रह्) takes the द्वितीया बहुवचन असद्ग्राहान् — 'false notions', literally 'unreal-graspings'. The double-grasp (gṛhītvā grāhān) is etymologically deliberate — they grasp graspings. प्रवर्तन्ते (आत्मनेपद, लट्, 'they set themselves in motion') completes the cycle: moved from within by what they have seized from without.

16.11.title: प्रलयान्ताम् — anxiety unto dissolution
16.11.body: चिन्ताम् अपरिमेयाम् प्रलयान्ताम् — three accusatives stack on the object of उपाश्रिताः ('having recourse to'): worry that is immeasurable and lasting until pralaya. प्रलयान्ताम् is a bahuvrīhi ('having pralaya as its end'), naming the demonic horizon: anxiety stretches not to death but to cosmic dissolution.

16.11.title: एतावत् इति निश्चिताः — the verdict of finitude
16.11.body: एतावत् ('just this much') with इति (the quotation marker) frames their conviction. निश्चिताः is past passive participle ('determined, convinced'). कामोपभोगपरमाः is a bahuvrīhi ('those for whom pleasure-enjoyment is the highest'). The grammatical strategy is elegant: their entire worldview is packaged into the quoted phrase 'only this much' — Sanskrit lets a single इति enclose an entire metaphysics.

16.12.title: आशापाशशतैः — instrumental of bondage
16.12.body: आशापाशशतैः is तृतीया बहुवचन ('by hundreds of nooses of hope') — a triple compound that is itself a chain of compounds, the grammar enacting the entanglement. बद्धाः (past passive participle, 'bound') is what one would expect of a finite verb, but here functions as the predicate adjective. Bondage is not done to them once; it is their state.

16.12.title: अन्यायेन — the instrumental of injustice
16.12.body: ईहन्ते (आत्मनेपद, लट्, 'they strive for') governs अर्थसञ्चयान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन, 'accumulations of wealth'). The means is given in तृतीया: अन्यायेन ('by unjust means'). Sanskrit's instrumental case carries no moral charge of its own — the same विभक्ति that names the tool of yoga here names the tool of theft. Context, not case, marks the ethics.

16.13.title: इदम् … इदम् … इदम् — the demonic monologue begins
16.13.body: The verse drops into direct interior speech: इदम् अद्य मया लब्धम् — 'this today by me obtained'. मया is तृतीया (the agent in a passive construction), लब्धम् is past passive participle of √लभ्. प्राप्स्ये (लृट्, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन, आत्मनेपद, from प्र + √आप्) is the future 'I shall obtain' — note the -स्य- infix as the लृट् signal.

16.13.title: भविष्यति — wealth that 'will be' mine
16.13.body: भविष्यति (√भू, लृट्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन) projects accumulation into the future, while अस्ति (लट्) anchors the present. The triple temporal grammar — अद्य present possession, लट् present existence, लृट् future acquisition — captures the demonic mind exhaustively claiming all three tenses for itself.

16.14.title: हतः … हनिष्ये — past kill, future kill
16.14.body: असौ मया हतः शत्रुः — पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा with हतः as past passive participle (√हन्), मया तृतीया. Then हनिष्ये (लृट्, आत्मनेपद-flavored, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन, 'I shall kill') with अपरान् अपि (द्वितीया बहुवचन, 'others too'). The same root √हन् stretched across past and future tense — the demonic identifies itself through killing's continuity.

16.14.title: ईश्वरः अहम् — the self-coronation
16.14.body: Five nominative predications in cascade: ईश्वरः अहम्, अहं भोगी, सिद्धः अहम्, बलवान्, सुखी. Each is पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन, the copula अस्मि implied. The grammatical economy of nominal sentences (no verb needed when both sides are nominatives) is here weaponized — five self-claims in twelve syllables, with no verb's resistance.

16.15.title: कः अन्यः अस्ति सदृशो मया — the rhetorical zero
16.15.body: कः अन्यः ('who else') with अस्ति (√अस्, लट्) and the comparison सदृशो मया (तृतीया with सदृश: 'similar to me') stages an unanswerable question. The grammar of comparison demands an instrumental of standard — here मया, meaning the speaker has installed himself as the standard against which all others fail.

16.15.title: यक्ष्ये दास्यामि मोदिष्ये — three futures of self-display
16.15.body: Three लृट् verbs in sequence: यक्ष्ये (√यज्, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन, आत्मनेपद, 'I shall sacrifice'), दास्यामि (√दा, परस्मैपद, 'I shall give'), मोदिष्ये (√मुद्, आत्मनेपद, 'I shall rejoice'). The -ष्य- infix signals all three. इति seals the speech, and अज्ञानविमोहिताः (past passive participle bahuvrīhi) labels the speakers — deluded by ignorance.

16.16.title: मोहजालसमावृताः — net-image as compound
16.16.body: Three predicate adjectives describe them: अनेकचित्तविभ्रान्ताः ('confused by many thoughts'), मोहजालसमावृताः ('enveloped by the net of delusion'), प्रसक्ताः ('clinging'). Each is a multi-stem compound functioning as a single predicate — Sanskrit's compounding here mimics the entanglement it describes: the words themselves are nets.

16.16.title: पतन्ति नरके अशुचौ — the finite verb arrives
16.16.body: After the predicate-adjective stack, the finite verb पतन्ति (√पत्, लट्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन, 'they fall') resolves the suspense. नरके अशुचौ is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in the impure hell'). The locative of destination is striking — they don't merely go to hell, they fall *within* it, the case suggesting they were already in its precinct.

16.17.title: आत्मसम्भाविताः — self-honored
16.17.body: आत्मसम्भाविताः is a tatpuruṣa with आत्मन् in तृतीया-sense: 'honored by themselves'. स्तब्धाः ('stiff, unbending') and धनमानमदान्विताः ('endowed with intoxication of wealth-and-pride') stack. The grammar layers a self-loop: the agent and object of esteem collapse into a single word.

16.17.title: नामयज्ञैः अविधिपूर्वकम् — sacrifices in name only
16.17.body: यजन्ते (√यज्, आत्मनेपद, लट्, बहुवचन, 'they sacrifice') governs तृतीया नामयज्ञैः ('with sacrifices that are sacrifices in name'). नाम as the first member of a karmadhāraya turns the noun into a hollowed-out label. अविधिपूर्वकम् is an adverbial accusative ('contrary to rule') — the very grammar of ritual is violated.

16.18.title: अहङ्कारं बलं दर्पम् — accusatives of refuge
16.18.body: Five द्वितीया objects line up: अहङ्कारम्, बलम्, दर्पम्, कामम्, क्रोधम्, all governed by संश्रिताः (past passive participle, 'taken refuge in'). Sanskrit lets a past passive participle govern accusatives when it retains active sense — these have *actively* clung to these qualities. The list rehearses the six āsurī attributes of 16.4 in a denser register.

16.18.title: मां प्रद्विषन्तः — the verb of hatred toward the divine
16.18.body: प्रद्विषन्तः (present participle, परस्मैपद, प्रथमा बहुवचन of प्र + √द्विष्) takes the accusative माम् — they hate Me. The locative आत्मपरदेहेषु ('in their own and others' bodies') localizes the hatred: Kṛṣṇa is the indweller in every body, so the demonic hatred is structurally suicidal, directed at the very ground they stand on.

16.19.title: क्षिपामि — Kṛṣṇa's own first-person verb
16.19.body: क्षिपामि (√क्षिप्, लट्, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन, 'I hurl') is the Lord's direct action. तान् द्विषतः क्रूरान् नराधमान् are four accusatives in apposition (the present participle द्विषतः agreeing with तान् as masc. acc. pl.). अजस्रम् is adverbial ('ceaselessly'). The chapter's only divine action verb is one of casting down.

16.19.title: आसुरीषु योनिषु — locative of demonic birth
16.19.body: आसुरीषु एव योनिषु is सप्तमी बहुवचन ('into demonic wombs only'), with एव sealing the exclusivity. Sanskrit uses सप्तमी for both 'in' and 'into' with verbs of casting — the case ending alone signals the trajectory's terminus. The wombs are not just where they go but where they belong.

16.20.title: जन्मनि जन्मनि — the locative of repetition
16.20.body: जन्मनि जन्मनि is सप्तमी एकवचन doubled — Sanskrit's vīpsā construction, the repeated locative meaning 'birth after birth'. आपन्नाः (past passive participle, 'having attained') and मूढाः (past passive participle, 'deluded') stack as predicates. Time itself becomes a locative the demonic cannot escape.

16.20.title: माम् अप्राप्य — the negative absolutive of failure
16.20.body: अप्राप्य is the negative absolutive (अ + प्र + आप् + ल्यप्), 'without attaining Me'. Because the upasarga प्र is present, the suffix is ल्यप्, not क्त्वा — the form is therefore ल्यबन्त, parallel in sense (but not in suffix) to Arjuna's क्त्वान्त अहत्वा at 2.5. यान्ति (√या, लट्) takes the द्वितीया अधमां गतिम् ('the lowest course'). The एव intensifies the impossibility — never reaching Him, always going lower.

16.21.title: त्रिविधं नरकस्य द्वारम् — the famous three gates
16.21.body: त्रिविधम् (नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा) qualifies द्वारम्, with नरकस्य (षष्ठी) as the genitive of belonging. आत्मनः नाशनम् ('destruction of the self') is in apposition. Then the three are named in three प्रथमा nominatives: कामः, क्रोधः, लोभः. The grammar mirrors the architecture: one gate, three leaves.

16.21.title: तस्मात् एतत् त्रयं त्यजेत् — the optative of duty
16.21.body: त्यजेत् is √त्यज्, विधिलिङ्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — 'one should abandon'. The विधिलिङ् mood is Sanskrit's home for ethical injunction (just as भुञ्जीय in 2.5 carried Arjuna's rhetorical horror). तस्मात् (पञ्चमी, 'therefore'), एतत् त्रयम् (नपुंसकलिङ्ग द्वितीया, 'this triad') — the verse hands the reader a single, optative-mood duty.

16.22.title: विमुक्तः — the past passive of release
16.22.body: विमुक्तः (past passive participle of वि + √मुच्) is पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन, agreeing with नरः. The तृतीया एतैः … त्रिभिः तमोद्वारैः ('by these three gates of darkness') marks the instrument of release-from — Sanskrit's तृतीया carries both 'by means of' and 'from' in the agent-of-a-passive-participle sense. He is released by/from these three.

16.22.title: आचरति … याति — the double finite verb
16.22.body: Two लट् verbs in sequence: आचरति (आ + √चर्, 'he practices') governs आत्मनः श्रेयः (षष्ठी + द्वितीया, 'what is good for the self'); याति (√या, 'he goes') governs परां गतिम् (द्वितीया, 'the supreme course'). The temporal ततः ('then, thereafter') sequences the two: practice precedes attainment, both in grammar and in life.

16.23.title: शास्त्रविधिम् उत्सृज्य — the absolutive of abandonment
16.23.body: उत्सृज्य is the absolutive (उद् + √सृज् + ल्यप्) — 'having abandoned'. The द्वितीया object शास्त्रविधिम् ('the injunction of śāstra') is what is cast off. वर्तते (√वृत्, आत्मनेपद, लट्, 'he conducts himself') governs कामकारतः (a tasil/pañcamī adverb from कामकार, 'by desire-doing'). The structure: abandon, then act on desire — the absolutive marks the moral sequence.

16.23.title: Triple न — denial of three rewards
16.23.body: न सिद्धिम् … न सुखम् … न परां गतिम् — three accusatives, each denied. अवाप्नोति (अव + √आप्, लट्) is the verb. The threefold negation echoes the threefold list of 16.7 — Sanskrit uses the rhetorical triple-न to enact exhaustive absence: not perfection, not happiness, not the supreme course. The gradation from worldly to ultimate is itself a teaching.

16.24.title: शास्त्रं प्रमाणम् — the nominal sentence of authority
16.24.body: तस्मात् शास्त्रं प्रमाणं ते is a verbless equational sentence: 'therefore śāstra (is) the standard for you'. ते is the enclitic form of युष्मद् (षष्ठी/चतुर्थी एकवचन). कार्याकार्यव्यवस्थितौ is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in the determination of what is to be done and what is not'): कार्य and अकार्य are कृत्य-प्रत्यय gerundives of √कृ joined in dvandva, and व्यवस्थिति is then bound to that pair as a tatpuruṣa. The locative case marks the domain of the standard's authority.

16.24.title: ज्ञात्वा … कर्तुम् अर्हसि — sequence the chapter closes on
16.24.body: ज्ञात्वा (absolutive of √ज्ञा, 'having known') governs the द्वितीया कर्म and the qualifier शास्त्रविधानोक्तम् (past passive participle compound, 'declared by the injunction of śāstra'). कर्तुम् is infinitive of √कृ ('to do'); अर्हसि (√अर्ह्, लट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन, 'you are fit') is the chapter's final finite verb. The closing grammar — know first, then act — repeats the entire pedagogy of the Gītā in one verse.
